name.text: Week 15 – Q3: Hertzian Dipole Validity and Design Correction

questiontext.text:
Scenario: A junior engineer at a wireless communications company is designing a compact antenna for a {@f_GHz@} GHz IoT (Internet of Things) sensor. They propose a {@l_cm@} cm dipole and want to use the Hertzian dipole model to predict its performance.

As the senior engineer reviewing this design, your first task is to check whether the simplified Hertzian dipole model is appropriate. If not, you must determine the maximum valid antenna length and evaluate its radiation properties.

The antenna parameters are:

  • Length: \(\ell = {@l_cm@}\) cm
  • Current amplitude: \(I_0 = {@I_0@}\) A
  • Operating frequency: \(f = {@f_GHz@}\) GHz
  • Medium: free space (\(\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\;\Omega\))

Part A — Validity Check

(a) Calculate the wavelength \(\lambda\) at the operating frequency.

\(\lambda = \) [[input:ans1]] [[validation:ans1]] m

Enter in metres, e.g., 0.234.

(b) Calculate the ratio \(\ell/\lambda\) to assess whether the antenna is electrically short.

\(\ell/\lambda = \) [[input:ans2]] [[validation:ans2]]

Enter a dimensionless number, e.g., 0.29.

(c) The Hertzian dipole model requires \(\ell/\lambda < 0.1\) (the antenna must be much shorter than the wavelength for the uniform-current assumption to hold). Is this model valid for the proposed antenna?

[[input:ans3]] [[validation:ans3]]

Part B — Design Correction

Since the Hertzian model is not valid for the proposed {@l_cm@} cm antenna, you advise the junior engineer to shorten it.

(d) What is the maximum antenna length \(\ell_{\max}\) (in cm) for which the Hertzian dipole model remains valid at this frequency?

\(\ell_{\max} = \) [[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]] cm

Enter in cm, e.g., 4.7.

Part C — Radiation Parameters at the Corrected Length

Using the corrected length \(\ell = \ell_{\max}\) from part (d), compute the radiation properties to assess whether this shorter antenna meets the design requirements.

(e) Radiation resistance: \(R_{\text{rad}} = 80\pi^2(\ell_{\max}/\lambda)^2\).

\(R_{\text{rad}} = \) [[input:ans5]] [[validation:ans5]] \(\Omega\)

Enter in ohms, e.g., 42.0.

(f) Total radiated power: \(P_{\text{rad}} = \frac{1}{2}I_0^2 R_{\text{rad}}\).

\(P_{\text{rad}} = \) [[input:ans6]] [[validation:ans6]] W

Enter in watts, e.g., 0.25.

(g) Directivity of a Hertzian dipole in dBi.

\(D = \) [[input:ans7]] [[validation:ans7]] dBi

Enter in dBi, e.g., 4.5.

Part D — Practical Implications

(h) The radiation resistance depends on the ratio \(\ell/\lambda\). If the antenna length were halved (from \(\ell_{\max}\) to \(\ell_{\max}/2\), keeping the same frequency), what would happen to \(R_{\text{rad}}\)?

[[input:ans8]] [[validation:ans8]]

(i) In practice, antennas also have ohmic (heat) losses. If this corrected antenna has a loss resistance of \(R_{\text{loss}} = {@R_loss@}\;\Omega\), what fraction of the input power is actually radiated?

Radiation efficiency \(\eta = R_{\text{rad}} / (R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}}) = \) [[input:ans9]] [[validation:ans9]]

Enter a number between 0 and 1, e.g., 0.29.

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Given: \(\ell = {@l_cm@}\) cm, \(f = {@f_GHz@}\) GHz, \(I_0 = {@I_0@}\) A.

(a) \(\lambda = c/f = 3\times10^8/{@f_Hz@} = {@tans1@}\) m = {@float(tans1*100)@} cm

(b) \(\ell/\lambda = {@l_cm@}/{@float(tans1*100)@} = {@tans2@}\)

(c) Since \(\ell/\lambda = {@tans2@} > 0.1\), the Hertzian dipole model is NOT valid. The antenna is too long relative to the wavelength for the uniform-current assumption to hold.

(d) Maximum valid length: \(\ell_{\max} = 0.1 \times \lambda = 0.1 \times {@float(tans1*100)@}\text{ cm} = {@tans4@}\) cm.

(e) At \(\ell = \ell_{\max}\), the ratio is \(\ell/\lambda = 0.1\), so:

\[R_{\text{rad}} = 80\pi^2(0.1)^2 = 0.8\pi^2 \approx 7.90\;\Omega\]

This is always the same at the validity boundary — a key insight about how small radiation resistance is for short dipoles.

(f) \(P_{\text{rad}} = \frac{1}{2} \times ({@I_0@})^2 \times 7.90 = {@tans6@}\) W

(g) \(D = 1.5 = 10\log_{10}(1.5) = 1.76\) dBi. This is the same for all Hertzian dipoles — directivity depends only on the \(\sin^2\theta\) pattern, not on length or frequency.

(h) Since \(R_{\text{rad}} = 80\pi^2(\ell/\lambda)^2 \propto \ell^2\), halving the length quarters the radiation resistance. At \(\ell_{\max}/2\): \(R_{\text{rad}} = 80\pi^2(0.05)^2 \approx 1.97\;\Omega\). This is a fundamental challenge for electrically short antennas: making them shorter dramatically reduces their ability to radiate.

(i) With \(R_{\text{rad}} \approx 7.90\;\Omega\) and \(R_{\text{loss}} = {@R_loss@}\;\Omega\):

\[\eta = \frac{R_{\text{rad}}}{R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}}} = \frac{7.90}{7.90 + {@R_loss@}} = {@tans9@}\]

Only {@float(tans9*100)@}% of the input power is radiated — the rest is lost as heat. This efficiency problem is why short antennas often need matching networks and low-loss materials to be practical.

questionnote.text: {@sconcat("l=", l_cm, "cm f=", f_GHz, "GHz I0=", I_0, "A ratio=", tans2, " lmax=", tans4, "cm eta=", tans9)@}

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Convert \(\ell\) to metres: {@l_cm@} cm = {@l_m@} m. Then \(\ell/\lambda\).

truefeedback.text: Correct. \(\ell/\lambda = {@tans2@} > 0.1\), so the Hertzian model does not apply.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. With \(\ell/\lambda = {@tans2@}\), which is greater than 0.1, the Hertzian dipole model is not valid. The uniform-current assumption fails when the antenna is not much shorter than \(\lambda\).
Incorrect. The criterion is \(\ell/\lambda < 0.1\), so the maximum length is \(\ell_{\max} = 0.1 \times \lambda\). Convert to cm.

truefeedback.text: Your \(R_{\text{rad}}\) is consistent with your \(\ell_{\max}\) from part (d) — correct formula, but \(\ell_{\max}\) was wrong. Half credit.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. \(R_{\text{rad}} = 80\pi^2(\ell_{\max}/\lambda)^2\). At the validity boundary \(\ell_{\max}/\lambda = 0.1\), this gives \(80\pi^2 \times 0.01 \approx 7.90\;\Omega\).

truefeedback.text: Your \(P_{\text{rad}}\) is consistent with your \(R_{\text{rad}}\) — correct method, but upstream error. Half credit.

feedbackvariables.text: lin_err: if is(abs(ans7 - D_linear)/abs(D_linear) < 0.06) then 1 else 0;

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Hertzian dipole directivity is always \(D = 1.5\). In dBi: \(10\log_{10}(1.5) = 1.76\).

truefeedback.text: Correct. \(R_{\text{rad}} \propto (\ell/\lambda)^2\), so halving \(\ell\) quarters \(R_{\text{rad}}\). This quadratic dependence is why electrically short antennas have very low radiation resistance.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Look at the formula: \(R_{\text{rad}} = 80\pi^2(\ell/\lambda)^2\). The length appears squared, so halving it reduces \(R_{\text{rad}}\) by a factor of \(2^2 = 4\).

feedbackvariables.text: /* Follow-through: efficiency from student's own R_rad */ ft_eta: if is(abs(ans5) > 0.001) then float(ans5 / (ans5 + R_loss)) else -999;

truefeedback.text: Correct. Only {@float(tans9*100)@}% of the input power is radiated — the rest is lost as heat in the antenna.
Correct formula (\(\eta = R_{\text{rad}}/(R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}})\)), but your \(R_{\text{rad}}\) from part (e) was not correct. Half credit for correct follow-through.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. \(\eta = R_{\text{rad}}/(R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}})\). Use your \(R_{\text{rad}}\) from part (e) and \(R_{\text{loss}} = {@R_loss@}\;\Omega\).

hint.text: Validity check: The Hertzian dipole model assumes uniform current along the antenna — this only holds when the antenna is much shorter than the wavelength. Compute \(\lambda = c/f\) and check whether \(\ell/\lambda < 0.1\). If the ratio exceeds 0.1, the current distribution becomes non-uniform and the simplified model breaks down.

Design correction: The maximum valid length is \(\ell_{\max} = 0.1\lambda\). At this boundary, the ratio \(\ell/\lambda\) is exactly 0.1, which simplifies the radiation resistance: \(R_{\text{rad}} = 80\pi^2(0.1)^2 = 0.8\pi^2 \approx 7.90\;\Omega\). Notice this result is independent of frequency — it depends only on the ratio.

Power and efficiency: Radiated power uses the peak current: \(P_{\text{rad}} = \frac{1}{2}I_0^2 R_{\text{rad}}\). Directivity is always 1.76 dBi for any Hertzian dipole. For radiation efficiency, think of \(R_{\text{rad}}\) and \(R_{\text{loss}}\) as a power divider: the fraction radiated is \(R_{\text{rad}}/(R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}})\).
